events.title: Mehrgarh
events.subtitle: First farmers of the Indus plain
events.body: Neolithic settlement in Balochistan — among the world's earliest farming communities. Evidence of mud-brick houses, wheat cultivation, and dental surgery.

events.title: Pottery & copper at Mehrgarh
events.subtitle: A village becomes a town
events.body: Wheel-made pottery emerges. Copper smelting begins. Mehrgarh grows denser, anticipating the urban revolution to come.

events.title: Harappa rises
events.subtitle: The first city of the Indus
events.body: On the banks of the Ravi, a planned city emerges with brick streets and granaries. The first chapter of one of the ancient world's three great river civilizations.

events.title: The Indus script appears
events.subtitle: A writing system still unread
events.body: Short inscriptions on seals — pictographs of bulls, fish, unicorns. After two centuries of effort, no scholar has deciphered them.

events.title: Mohenjo-daro at its peak
events.subtitle: 40,000 souls on the Indus
events.body: At its height, one of the largest cities on earth. Grid streets, indoor plumbing, uniform brick sizes across 1,000 km — a civilization without kings.

events.title: Trade with Mesopotamia
events.subtitle: Indus seals in Sumeria
events.body: Carnelian beads, lapis, and Indus seals turn up in Ur and Akkad. Sumerian texts speak of 'Meluhha' — almost certainly the Indus.

events.title: The Indus Valley empties
events.subtitle: Cities slowly abandoned
events.body: Rivers shift, monsoons weaken, trade collapses. Mohenjo-daro and Harappa thin out. The script disappears. Whatever the Indus knew of itself is lost.

events.title: Darius takes Sindh
events.subtitle: The Indus, an Achaemenid satrapy
events.body: Darius I extends the Persian Empire to the Indus. The 'Hindush' satrapy pays the largest tribute in gold of any province. Aramaic script and Persian coinage arrive.

events.title: Alexander at Taxila
events.subtitle: The Macedonian crosses the Indus
events.body: Alexander defeats Porus at the Hydaspes (Jhelum). His army mutinies on the Beas, refusing to march further east. He turns back through Makran — and never sees Macedon again.

events.title: Ashoka's edicts at Shahbazgarhi
events.subtitle: Buddhist law carved into rock
events.body: The Mauryan emperor inscribes his rock edicts in Kharosthi at Shahbazgarhi and Mansehra — preaching dhamma, non-violence, and tolerance to a frontier population.

events.title: Kanishka and Buddhist Gandhara
events.subtitle: Greco-Buddhist art flowers
events.body: Under the Kushan emperor Kanishka, Peshawar (Purushapura) becomes a great Buddhist capital. Gandharan sculptors fuse Hellenistic form with Indic devotion — the Buddha gets a face.

events.title: The White Huns descend
events.subtitle: Gandhara's golden age ends
events.body: Hephthalite raiders sweep through Gandhara, sacking monasteries and shrines. The Buddhist civilization of the northwest never recovers its old confidence.

events.title: Muhammad bin Qasim
events.subtitle: Arab general conquers Sindh
events.body: A 17-year-old Umayyad commander crosses the Indus, defeats Raja Dahir at the Battle of Aror. Islam arrives in the subcontinent not as a distant wind but a full landing.

events.title: First mosques at Multan
events.subtitle: An Arab city on the Chenab
events.body: Multan and Mansura become Arab provincial capitals. Coins struck in Sindh bear the Kalima. The Sun Temple of Multan still stands, drawing Hindu pilgrims under Muslim governors.

events.title: Ismaili Multan
events.subtitle: A Fatimid outpost on the Indus
events.body: Multan becomes a Fatimid Ismaili emirate, owing allegiance to Cairo. The khutba is read in the Fatimid caliph's name — until Mahmud of Ghazni rides south.

events.title: Mahmud of Ghazni
events.subtitle: Seventeen raids across the Indus
events.body: From his capital in Afghanistan, Mahmud launches seventeen campaigns into the subcontinent between 1001 and 1027. Somnath's great temple is sacked. Lahore becomes the easternmost outpost of his Turkic empire.

events.title: Al-Biruni writes the Kitab al-Hind
events.subtitle: An Arab scholar reads India whole
events.body: Travelling with Mahmud, the polymath Al-Biruni learns Sanskrit and composes the first serious study of Indian religion, science, and mathematics by an outsider.

events.title: Data Ganj Bakhsh in Lahore
events.subtitle: The Sufi arrives
events.body: Ali Hujwiri — Data Ganj Bakhsh — writes the Kashf al-Mahjub in Lahore, the first Persian treatise on Sufism. His shrine remains the oldest in the subcontinent and the beating heart of the city.

events.title: Muhammad Ghuri at Tarain
events.subtitle: The gate swings open
events.body: Muhammad Ghuri defeats Prithviraj Chauhan at the Second Battle of Tarain. The door to the Gangetic plain, closed for five centuries, is kicked in.

events.title: The Delhi Sultanate begins
events.subtitle: Qutb-ud-din Aibak takes the throne
events.body: A former slave, now sultan. The Mamluk dynasty begins a 320-year succession of Turkic and Afghan sultans ruling northern India from Delhi.

events.title: Baba Farid at Pakpattan
events.subtitle: Punjabi Sufism takes root
events.body: Fariduddin Ganjshakar teaches and writes in Punjabi — his verses later enter the Guru Granth Sahib. The Chishti order spreads across the land through his khanqah at Pakpattan.

events.title: Amir Khusrau
events.subtitle: The parrot of India sings
events.body: Poet, musician, historian — Khusrau fuses Persian and Hindavi, reportedly invents the tabla and the sitar, and crafts the qawwali tradition at the feet of Nizamuddin Auliya.

events.title: Timur sacks Delhi
events.subtitle: Three days of massacre
events.body: Timur crosses the Indus, defeats the Tughlaq army, and puts Delhi to the sword. The Sultanate never fully recovers. From his line will come, four generations later, Babur.

events.title: Guru Nanak in Punjab
events.subtitle: There is no Hindu, there is no Muslim
events.body: Nanak emerges from the river at Sultanpur with a new teaching — one God, universal, beyond sect. Sikhism is born in the Punjab that will become Pakistan.

events.title: Babur takes Panipat
events.subtitle: The Mughal era begins
events.body: A Central Asian prince descended from both Timur and Chingiz, bearing twenty cannon and a new faith in artillery, destroys Ibrahim Lodi's army. The Mughal empire is founded on the rubble of the Delhi Sultanate.

events.title: The Baburnama is compiled
events.subtitle: The first autobiography in the Islamic world
events.body: Babur dies in Agra leaving the Baburnama — a Chaghatai Turkish memoir of startling candour. No other founder of an empire has left so intimate an account of himself.

events.title: Humayun flees to Persia
events.subtitle: The empire nearly dies in its cradle
events.body: Sher Shah Suri, an Afghan governor of Bihar, defeats Humayun at Kannauj. The Mughal prince spends fifteen years in exile at the Safavid court. Sher Shah's five-year reign reorganizes the empire Humayun will return to reclaim.

events.title: Akbar takes the throne at thirteen
events.subtitle: Bairam Khan wins the second Panipat for his ward
events.body: Humayun dies falling down library stairs. His thirteen-year-old son is crowned on a brick platform in the Punjab. Within four years, Akbar is ruling in his own name — and within forty, he will build an empire his descendants cannot quite hold.

events.title: Akbar moves the capital to Lahore
events.subtitle: Thirteen years on the Ravi
events.body: Akbar rules the empire from Lahore for thirteen years, building the fort's Shah Burj and drawing poets, painters, and theologians to the city. Lahore, briefly, is the capital of the world's richest state.

events.title: Din-i-Ilahi
events.subtitle: Akbar's universal faith, open to ulema and Jesuits alike
events.body: In the Ibadat Khana at Fatehpur Sikri, Akbar has already hosted Jesuits, Jains, Zoroastrians, and ulama in open debate. He now proclaims a syncretic faith of his own. It will not survive him — but the instinct will shape two centuries of Mughal rule.

events.title: Jahangir marries Nur Jahan
events.subtitle: The empress behind the curtain
events.body: Nur Jahan — born Mehr-un-Nissa on a roadside in Kandahar — becomes the most powerful woman in Mughal history, signing imperial farmans and issuing coinage in her own name.

events.title: Shah Jahan ascends
events.subtitle: The builder-emperor
events.body: Khurram, now Shah Jahan, executes his rival brothers and takes the throne at Agra. Over thirty years, he will pour the empire's wealth into marble: the Taj Mahal, the Red Fort, the Shalimar Gardens of Lahore.

events.title: Shalimar Gardens at Lahore
events.subtitle: Paradise on three terraces
events.body: Shah Jahan's engineers carve a Persian paradise garden from Lahore's dust — 410 fountains fed by a canal from the Rachna doab, cypresses and mangoes in geometric ranks.

events.title: Aurangzeb defeats Dara Shikoh
events.subtitle: Orthodoxy wins the war of succession
events.body: Two brothers, two Mughal futures. Dara — the Sufi-inflected translator of the Upanishads. Aurangzeb — the austere Sunni warrior. Dara loses at Samugarh, is paraded through Delhi in rags, and beheaded. The synthesis Akbar built begins to crack.

events.title: Badshahi Mosque in Lahore
events.subtitle: The largest mosque in the world
events.body: Aurangzeb commissions a mosque to hold 55,000 worshippers — the red sandstone answer to his father's white marble. Completed in three years. It will remain the largest on earth for 313.

events.title: Aurangzeb in the Deccan
events.subtitle: Twenty-six years of campaigns empty the treasury
events.body: Aurangzeb leaves Delhi for the south and never returns. His army — slow, siege-heavy, bleeding money — grinds itself down against Maratha guerrillas for twenty-six years. The Mughal state is hollowed out from within before any outsider touches it.

events.title: Nadir Shah sacks Delhi
events.subtitle: The Peacock Throne carried to Iran
events.body: The Persian conqueror defeats Muhammad Shah at Karnal, enters Delhi, and over 57 hours slaughters tens of thousands. He leaves carrying the Peacock Throne and the Koh-i-Noor. The Mughal emperor continues to rule — but only in name.

events.title: Panipat, a third time
events.subtitle: Ahmad Shah Abdali breaks the Marathas
events.body: The Afghan king destroys the Maratha army at the Third Battle of Panipat. A hundred thousand die in a single day. The question of who will inherit Mughal India is left open — long enough for the East India Company to answer it.

events.title: Plassey
events.subtitle: A trading company wins a kingdom
events.body: Robert Clive defeats the Nawab of Bengal through bribery as much as battle. The East India Company, chartered to trade in pepper, is now a territorial power. The subcontinent's long colonial night begins.

events.title: The Chalisa Famine
events.subtitle: Punjab, Delhi, Rajputana starve
events.body: A failed monsoon and scorched-earth warfare between Marathas and Rohillas produce a famine across northern India. Villages in the Punjab are abandoned. Up to eleven million die in the subcontinent over two years.

events.title: Ghalib is born
events.subtitle: In Agra, the last great poet of the Mughal twilight
events.body: Mirza Asadullah Khan Ghalib is born in Agra to a Turkic family of soldiers. He will spend most of his life in Delhi, write in Urdu and Persian, watch the Mughal capital destroyed by the British in 1857, and die pensionless in 1869. The couplets carry the Urdu language out of the ruins.

events.title: Ranjit Singh crowned Maharaja
events.subtitle: A Sikh empire from the Sutlej to Peshawar
events.body: The one-eyed Lion of Punjab consolidates the misls into a single state. For forty years, Lahore is the capital of an empire reaching from the Khyber to Kashmir — the last indigenous great power west of the Sutlej.

events.title: First Anglo-Sikh War
events.subtitle: The Khalsa meets the Company
events.body: The Sikh empire, six years without Ranjit Singh, is at war with itself. The British press the Sutlej frontier. By 1849, after a second war, the Punjab is annexed and Duleep Singh — ten years old — is shipped to England with the Koh-i-Noor.

events.subtitle: Delhi falls and the last Mughal is exiled to Rangoon
events.body: Sepoys mutiny over greased cartridges; the fire sweeps from Meerut to Delhi, Lucknow, Kanpur. Bahadur Shah Zafar, the last Mughal, is declared emperor again for four months. The British crush the revolt, exile Zafar to Rangoon, and dissolve the Company. The Crown takes India.

events.title: The Great White Revenge
events.subtitle: Delhi and Awadh under mass reprisal
events.body: British columns enter Delhi in September 1857. The city is given over to the troops. Homes are looted. Mosques are seized. Mughal princes are shot at the Khooni Darwaza; the last emperor's sons' heads are delivered to him on a platter. Villages along the Ganga are put to the torch. Hangings, cannon-mouth executions, the humiliation of the defeated as spectacle. The British Empire's official histories call it 'the mutiny'; the population of northern India remembers otherwise.

events.title: The Raj begins
events.subtitle: The Queen's Proclamation; the Company is dissolved
events.body: Parliament passes the Government of India Act. The East India Company — chartered 1600, ruler of the subcontinent for a century — is dissolved. Queen Victoria becomes sovereign. A Viceroy replaces the Governor-General. The Mughal pretext is gone: the subcontinent is now, openly, a Crown possession.

events.title: The Upper India Famine
events.subtitle: Punjab and the NWFP under drought
events.body: The first great famine of the Crown era. Punjab, Delhi, and the North-West Provinces lose perhaps two million. Officials debate whether the Company's old grain policies should have been kept.

events.title: The Bihar famine
events.subtitle: A famine prevented by a Lieutenant-Governor
events.body: Monsoon failure in Bihar and eastern Bengal threatens mass starvation. Sir Richard Temple moves aggressively — Burmese rice imports, public works, free relief. Perhaps 100,000 die where millions were at risk. London rebukes him for spending too much. The lesson the Raj takes: next time, spend less.

events.title: The Great Famine
events.subtitle: Five million dead across the south and east
events.body: Chastened by London, Temple now imposes the 'Temple wage' — a ration lower than Auschwitz's. Drought across Madras, Bombay, and Bengal becomes famine. Grain exports to Britain continue throughout. Between five and ten million die. Free-market orthodoxy, defended by the Viceroy Lytton, becomes the policy of the Raj.

events.title: Sir Syed founds Aligarh
events.subtitle: Modern Muslim education begins
events.body: Sir Syed Ahmed Khan opens the Muhammadan Anglo-Oriental College at Aligarh — English-medium, scientific, self-consciously Muslim. A generation of its graduates will found the Muslim League.

events.title: The Indian famine of 1896
events.subtitle: Grain rots in railheads while villages starve
events.body: Failed monsoons across the Central Provinces, Bengal, and the Punjab. Official count: five million dead. The railways move grain out of famine zones to paying customers on the coast. Sir Richard Temple's doctrine — that relief must not compete with private trade — is still policy.

events.title: Muslim League founded at Dhaka
events.subtitle: A separate political voice
events.body: At Dhaka's Shahbagh, the All-India Muslim League is born — to safeguard Muslim political rights under the Raj. It will, within forty years, partition a subcontinent.

events.title: Lucknow Pact
events.subtitle: Jinnah brokers Hindu-Muslim unity
events.body: A young Jinnah — still a Congress member — negotiates an accord between Congress and the League on separate electorates. Sarojini Naidu calls him 'the ambassador of Hindu-Muslim unity.'

events.title: Jallianwala Bagh
events.subtitle: Amritsar, April 13
events.body: General Dyer orders his troops to fire into a walled garden packed with Baisakhi pilgrims. Official count: 379 dead. Unofficial: over a thousand. The moral case for the Raj never recovers.

events.title: Iqbal's Allahabad Address
events.subtitle: A Muslim state in the northwest
events.body: Presiding over the Muslim League, Allama Iqbal calls for 'the formation of a consolidated North-West Indian Muslim state' — the first major articulation of what will become Pakistan.

events.title: 'Pakistan' is coined
events.subtitle: A pamphlet from Cambridge
events.body: Choudhry Rahmat Ali circulates 'Now or Never' at Cambridge, coining the name from Punjab, Afghania, Kashmir, Sindh, and Balochistan. P-A-K-S-T-A-N. He adds an 'i' for euphony.

events.title: Lahore Resolution
events.subtitle: The demand formalized
events.body: At Minto Park, the Muslim League resolves that Muslim-majority regions in the northwest and east should be 'independent states.' What was Iqbal's idea and Rahmat Ali's slogan is now party policy.

events.title: The Bengal Famine
events.subtitle: Three million dead in the Raj's last great famine
events.body: War diversions, rice exports, and the denial policy against a feared Japanese advance combine with a cyclone to produce famine in Bengal. Up to three million die. Churchill's wartime cabinet refuses to release grain. The memory shapes Bengali politics into Pakistan's eastern wing and beyond.

events.title: Muslim League sweeps
events.subtitle: A mandate for Pakistan
events.body: In the central legislative elections, the League wins every Muslim seat. In the provincial elections, 87% of Muslim seats. The claim to speak for India's Muslims is, electorally, answered.

events.title: The Mountbatten Plan
events.subtitle: Partition in ten weeks
events.body: The Viceroy announces that India will be divided and independent by August 15. A British judge, Cyril Radcliffe, who has never seen India, is given five weeks to draw the border through Bengal and Punjab.

events.title: Direct Action Day
events.subtitle: The Great Calcutta Killings
events.body: Jinnah calls for a day of direct action to press the demand for Pakistan. In Calcutta, four days of communal rioting leave 4,000 dead and 100,000 homeless. Violence crosses into Noakhali, then Bihar, then the Punjab. The prospect of a united India is now a fiction.

events.title: Jinnah to the Constituent Assembly
events.subtitle: You are free
events.body: 'You are free; you are free to go to your temples, you are free to go to your mosques or to any other place of worship.' Three days before Pakistan exists, its founder tells it what he wants it to be.

events.title: Pakistan
events.subtitle: We were bloody, but here we were free
events.body: A country of 75 million is born, in two wings separated by a thousand miles of hostile India. Jinnah takes the oath in Karachi. Within weeks, a million will die and fifteen million be displaced in the partition migrations. It is the largest migration in human history.

events.title: The Radcliffe Line is published
events.subtitle: A border released two days after independence
events.body: Cyril Radcliffe's award is made public after the two states already exist. Gurdaspur goes to India; Chittagong Hill Tracts, 97% non-Muslim, to Pakistan. Villages learn overnight which country they now live in. The bureaucratic line through Punjab and Bengal becomes the knife-edge of the migrations.

events.title: The Punjab migrations
events.subtitle: Fifteen million move, a million die
events.body: Trains arrive at Lahore and Amritsar stations carrying only corpses. Sikh jathas strike eastbound Muslim columns; Muslim mobs strike westbound Hindu and Sikh columns. The Punjab Boundary Force — 50,000 men — is overwhelmed within days. By November the exchange is mostly done. The number killed is commonly set at a million; no one counted.

events.title: Junagadh accedes to Pakistan
events.subtitle: A Muslim prince, a Hindu subject people
events.body: The Nawab of Junagadh — a Kathiawar state with an 80% Hindu population — signs the instrument of accession to Pakistan. Jinnah accepts. India blockades the state, stages a 'referendum' after Indian troops enter, and annexes it in November. The logic India uses here — ruler's wishes irrelevant, demography decisive — will be inverted for Kashmir.

events.title: Tribal lashkars enter Kashmir
events.subtitle: Pashtun irregulars cross the Jhelum
events.body: Pashtun tribesmen from the NWFP — armed, fuelled, and quietly directed by Pakistani officers — cross into Kashmir to pre-empt a Dogra accession to India. They reach Baramulla within days, then stop to loot. The delay decides the war. Srinagar is saved.

events.title: Maharaja Hari Singh signs
events.subtitle: Kashmir accedes to India
events.body: His state falling, the Hindu Maharaja of a Muslim-majority Kashmir signs the Instrument of Accession to India. Mountbatten accepts — conditionally, pending a 'reference to the people' once order is restored. That reference has not happened.

events.title: Indian troops airlift to Srinagar
events.subtitle: The first Kashmir war begins
events.body: Within 24 hours of the accession, Indian infantry is landing at Srinagar airfield. The tribal advance is halted at Shalateng. Liaquat orders Pakistan's regular army in — Auchinleck, still Supreme Commander, threatens to withdraw every British officer from both armies. Liaquat backs down; irregulars and volunteers carry the fight. The line they freeze on becomes the LoC.

events.title: Hyderabad's standstill agreement
events.subtitle: A Muslim Nizam tries to stay independent
events.body: The Nizam of Hyderabad — ruling a landlocked, 85% Hindu state the size of France — signs a one-year standstill with India while negotiating for sovereignty. He raises an irregular force, the Razakars, under Qasim Razvi. India prepares for a different outcome.

events.title: Operation Polo
events.subtitle: Hyderabad annexed in five days
events.body: Indian armour enters Hyderabad State. The Nizam's forces surrender in under a week. Official Indian count of the communal violence that follows: 27,000 to 40,000 Muslim dead. The Sunderlal Report documenting it is suppressed for half a century.

events.title: Liaquat's first cabinet
events.subtitle: Seven ministers, a Hindu among them
events.body: Liaquat Ali Khan is sworn in as Prime Minister with six colleagues: Zafrullah Khan (Foreign), Ghulam Muhammad (Finance), Fazlur Rahman (Interior & Information), Ibrahim Ismail Chundrigar (Commerce), Abdur Rab Nishtar (Communications), and Jogendra Nath Mandal — a Scheduled Caste Hindu — as Law Minister. Pakistan opens as a plural state.

events.title: First Kashmir War
events.subtitle: Ceasefire at the Line of Control
events.body: Tribal lashkars from the NWFP cross into Kashmir. The Maharaja accedes to India; Indian troops airlift into Srinagar. A UN ceasefire in January 1949 draws the Line of Control — and leaves Kashmir unresolved for every decade since.

events.title: Jinnah dies
events.subtitle: Thirteen months after creation
events.body: The Quaid, sick with tuberculosis that had been kept secret through partition, dies in a broken-down ambulance outside Karachi. The state he built is barely a year old.

events.title: Objectives Resolution
events.subtitle: Sovereignty belongs to Allah
events.body: The Constituent Assembly passes the Objectives Resolution, declaring that sovereignty over the universe belongs to Allah and is to be exercised through the people within limits He has prescribed. The first formal departure from Jinnah's secular vision.

events.title: Mandal resigns
events.subtitle: The Law Minister leaves for Calcutta
events.body: Jogendra Nath Mandal — the Scheduled Caste Hindu Jinnah had placed in his first cabinet as a sign of the state's plural intent — sends his resignation from Calcutta, citing the treatment of Hindu minorities in East Bengal. The founding cabinet's inclusive gesture is over.

events.title: Liaquat Ali Khan shot
events.subtitle: Company Bagh, Rawalpindi
events.body: Pakistan's first Prime Minister is killed at a public meeting. His assassin — an Afghan named Said Akbar — is shot dead on the spot. The motive will never be established. Khwaja Nazimuddin, Governor-General, steps down to become PM.

events.title: Nazimuddin dismissed
events.subtitle: Ghulam Muhammad's constitutional coup
events.body: Khwaja Nazimuddin commands a majority in the Constituent Assembly. Governor-General Ghulam Muhammad dismisses him anyway, citing the anti-Ahmadi riots in Punjab. No court stops it. The precedent — that the executive can unmake an elected PM — is laid down before the 1956 Constitution is written.

events.title: Pakistan joins SEATO and CENTO
events.subtitle: Entering the Western alliance system
events.body: In exchange for military aid, Pakistan signs on to the two Western pacts ringing the Soviet Union. The alignment will define foreign policy, and the army's weight within it, for decades.

events.title: One Unit
events.subtitle: The four western provinces merged
events.body: Punjab, Sindh, NWFP, Balochistan, and the princely states of West Pakistan are fused into a single 'West Pakistan' province — framed as administrative reform, understood in smaller provinces as Punjabi consolidation. The resentment feeds every later regionalist movement.

events.title: The 1956 Constitution
events.subtitle: An Islamic Republic
events.body: After nine years of drafting and redrafting, Pakistan finally gets a constitution. It declares an 'Islamic Republic' — a formula Jinnah never used. It will last two years.

events.title: Ayub Khan's coup
events.subtitle: The army takes over
events.body: President Iskandar Mirza abrogates the constitution and appoints General Ayub Khan Chief Martial Law Administrator. Three weeks later Ayub dispatches Mirza to London and keeps the country. The pattern recurs — 1977, 1999.

events.title: The Decade of Development
events.subtitle: Tarbela, Mangla, the twenty-two families
events.body: Ayub's technocrats pour aid money into Green Revolution wheat, the Tarbela and Mangla dams, West Pakistani industry. GDP climbs. Inequality climbs faster. East Pakistan grows poorer. Twenty-two families, famously, come to own two-thirds of industry.

events.title: The 1965 War
events.subtitle: Seventeen days over Kashmir
events.body: Operation Gibraltar sends irregulars into Kashmir; India counter-attacks across the international border toward Lahore. A UN-brokered ceasefire follows. Both sides claim victory. The war ends Ayub's aura of competence.

events.title: Tashkent Declaration
events.subtitle: Shastri dies, Bhutto resigns
events.body: Ayub and Shastri sign a Soviet-brokered peace. Shastri dies of a heart attack that night. Ayub's young foreign minister — Zulfikar Ali Bhutto — resigns in protest and emerges as his great rival.

events.title: Yahya Khan takes over
events.subtitle: Martial law for the second time
events.body: Facing months of mass protests, Ayub hands power not to the Speaker of the Assembly, as the constitution requires, but to his army chief Yahya Khan. Martial law is declared, One Unit is dissolved, a new election is promised.

events.title: The 1970 election
events.subtitle: Mujib in the East, Bhutto in the West
events.body: Pakistan's first general election on universal franchise. Mujibur Rahman's Awami League sweeps East Pakistan — 160 of 162 seats, an outright majority in the national assembly. Bhutto's PPP wins the West. Neither side will hand power to the other.

events.title: Operation Searchlight
events.subtitle: The army moves against East Pakistan
events.body: Tikka Khan's troops launch a pre-planned crackdown on Dhaka — Hindu neighbourhoods, the university, Awami League cadres. Mujib is arrested and flown west. By year's end, between 300,000 and three million East Pakistanis are dead and ten million have fled to India.

events.title: Fall of Dhaka
events.subtitle: The eastern wing is lost
events.body: General A. A. K. Niazi surrenders to Lieutenant-General Aurora at the Dhaka racecourse. 93,000 Pakistani soldiers go into Indian captivity. Pakistan loses its majority population, its eastern wing, and the Two-Nation Theory as a tidy proposition.

events.title: Bhutto takes over
events.subtitle: Civilian rule returns to a rump state
events.body: Four days after Dhaka falls, Yahya hands power to Zulfikar Ali Bhutto — civilian, charismatic, brilliant, authoritarian. A broken army, a humiliated country, and ninety thousand prisoners in Indian camps wait for him to act.

events.title: Balochistan government dismissed
events.subtitle: Bhutto removes the NAP ministry
events.body: Bhutto sacks the elected NAP-JUI coalition government in Balochistan under Ataullah Mengal, citing a cache of Iraqi weapons recovered from the Iraqi embassy. The KP government of Mufti Mahmud resigns in protest. A Baloch insurgency begins that will run for four years.

events.title: The 1973 Constitution
events.subtitle: A federal parliamentary framework
events.body: Drawn up by consensus across parties, the 1973 constitution establishes a parliamentary system with a bicameral legislature. Amended, suspended, and partly restored many times — it remains, nominally, in force today.

events.title: Ahmadis declared non-Muslim
events.subtitle: The Second Amendment
events.body: Parliament amends the constitution to declare members of the Ahmadiyya community non-Muslim. Bhutto, the self-described socialist, writes sectarian exclusion into the state's basic law.

events.title: The FSF and the Kasuri killing
events.subtitle: Ahmed Raza's father shot in Lahore
events.body: The Federal Security Force — Bhutto's paramilitary — attempts to assassinate the PPP dissident Ahmed Raza Kasuri near his house in Lahore's Shadman. The attackers miss; his father, Nawab Muhammad Ahmad Khan, is killed in the car beside him. The case will return, three years later, to send Bhutto to the gallows.

events.title: Hayat Sherpao assassinated
events.subtitle: A bomb at Peshawar University
events.body: PPP stalwart and KP governor Hayat Sherpao — Bhutto's closest friend in the party — is killed by a bomb at a gathering at Peshawar University. Bhutto blames the National Awami Party. The next day, NAP is banned.

events.title: The NAP is destroyed
events.subtitle: Wali Khan and the Hyderabad Tribunal
events.body: Wali Khan, Ghaus Bakhsh Bizenjo, Attaullah Mengal, Khair Bakhsh Marri, and Ajmal Khattak are arrested. The Supreme Court dissolves the NAP in October. A special tribunal at Hyderabad tries them for treason. The country's largest Pashtun-Baloch opposition is removed from politics by decree.

events.title: Zia overthrows Bhutto
events.subtitle: Operation Fair Play
events.body: Chief of Army Staff Zia-ul-Haq, personally chosen by Bhutto for his seeming mildness, arrests the Prime Minister. Martial law. Ninety-day elections promised. They come eleven years later.

events.title: Rahimuddin ends the Baloch insurgency
events.subtitle: Amnesty and isolation under Zia
events.body: Zia names General Rahimuddin Khan Governor of Balochistan. He offers a general amnesty, releases Hyderabad Tribunal prisoners, and withdraws the army from mountain operations — while cutting the province off from national politics for six years. The insurgency Bhutto started ends without settlement.

events.title: Bhutto hanged
events.subtitle: Rawalpindi jail, before dawn
events.body: Convicted of conspiracy to murder a political opponent in a trial widely regarded as a judicial killing. Zia refuses international clemency appeals. The PPP never forgets. Benazir is twenty-five.

events.title: Soviets invade Afghanistan
events.subtitle: Pakistan becomes a frontline state again
events.body: The Red Army crosses the Oxus. Overnight, Zia goes from pariah military dictator to pivotal ally. Billions of dollars and weapons flow through the ISI to the Afghan mujahideen. The pipeline reshapes Pakistan — and doesn't close when Moscow leaves.

events.title: Zia's Islamization
events.subtitle: Hudood, zakat, blasphemy
events.body: Hudood Ordinances criminalize zina. Federal Shariat Court established. Blasphemy clauses 295-B and 295-C added to the penal code with mandatory penalties. The state's relationship with religion is rewritten in five years.

events.title: Zia's C-130 falls from the sky
events.subtitle: Near Bahawalpur
events.body: Zia, the US Ambassador Raphel, General Akhtar, and 27 others die when a C-130 crashes after take-off. Sabotage is suspected. No inquiry is allowed to reach any conclusion.

events.title: Benazir Bhutto becomes PM
events.subtitle: The first woman to lead a Muslim-majority state
events.body: At 35, Benazir sweeps in on her father's legacy. Boxed in by a hostile president (Ghulam Ishaq Khan), a hostile army, and a hostile opposition led by Nawaz Sharif. Dismissed within two years on corruption charges.

events.title: Benazir dismissed
events.subtitle: President Ghulam Ishaq Khan invokes 58(2)(b)
events.body: Twenty months in, President Ghulam Ishaq Khan dismisses the Benazir government under the Eighth Amendment's 58(2)(b) — corruption, incompetence, failure to maintain law and order. The Supreme Court upholds the dismissal.

events.title: Nawaz Sharif, first term
events.subtitle: The Punjab industrialist takes Islamabad
events.body: Nawaz — Zia's protégé, Lahori steel magnate — forms a government from the IJI. Begins liberalization: denationalizations, motorways, a yellow-cab scheme.

events.title: Nawaz resigns
events.subtitle: The Kakar formula dissolves both offices
events.body: Ghulam Ishaq Khan dismisses Nawaz; the Supreme Court reinstates him. President and PM now open warfare on each other. Army Chief Abdul Waheed Kakar brokers a deal: both men resign. An interim government calls elections.

events.title: Benazir returns
events.subtitle: A second term
events.body: Benazir wins again. Her husband Asif Zardari — 'Mr Ten Percent' — quickly attracts the corruption cases. Her brother Murtaza, who thinks the PPP should have been his, is killed by police in Karachi in September 1996.

events.title: Benazir dismissed again
events.subtitle: President Leghari invokes 58(2)(b)
events.body: Farooq Leghari — Benazir's own nominee to the presidency — dismisses her government. Corruption, extrajudicial killings in Karachi, the death of Murtaza. Second time in eight years Benazir is sent home mid-term.

events.title: Nawaz returns with a heavy mandate
events.subtitle: Two-thirds majority, thirteenth amendment
events.body: PML-N sweeps the 1997 election. Nawaz repeals the Eighth Amendment's 58(2)(b), stripping the president's power to dismiss the PM. Declares an end to 'horse-trading'. Within two years he will try to sack the army chief.

events.title: Chagai
events.subtitle: Pakistan goes nuclear
events.body: Seventeen days after India's Pokhran-II tests, Nawaz Sharif orders Pakistan's answer. Five devices detonated in the Ras Koh hills of Balochistan. A sixth two days later. South Asia is now a nuclear theatre.

events.title: Kargil
events.subtitle: Pakistani troops in Indian positions across the Line of Control
events.body: Pakistani troops infiltrate Indian positions in the Kargil-Dras sector. Nawaz Sharif claims he wasn't told. Chief of Army Staff Pervez Musharraf claims he was. A full conventional war under the nuclear ceiling is narrowly avoided. Sharif flies to Washington and asks Clinton to help him back down.

events.title: Musharraf's coup
events.subtitle: A PIA flight denied landing
events.body: Nawaz tries to sack Musharraf mid-flight and refuses his Karachi-bound PIA plane landing permission. The Corps Commanders move. By nightfall the government has fallen. Nawaz is later exiled to Saudi Arabia. Musharraf becomes Chief Executive.

events.title: After 9/11
events.subtitle: Pakistan enters the War on Terror
events.body: Musharraf receives a call from Richard Armitage and a demand list from Washington. Pakistan, three weeks a sponsor of the Taliban, becomes a US ally in the War on Terror. Airspace, bases, intelligence — granted. The Taliban leadership slips across the Durand Line into Quetta.

events.title: Local bodies empowered
events.subtitle: Musharraf devolves authority to the districts
events.body: The Local Government Ordinance 2001 establishes directly elected Nazims at district, tehsil, and union level, with fiscal and administrative powers transferred from the provincial civil service. Critics call it a military regime sidelining the provinces; supporters call it the deepest decentralization Pakistan has ever attempted. Most of it is rolled back after Musharraf falls.

events.title: Lal Masjid
events.subtitle: Operation Silence
events.body: After months of vigilantism by students of the Red Mosque in Islamabad, commandos storm the complex. Over a hundred die, including the cleric leader. The TTP forms in reaction. A new insurgency opens in the tribal areas.

events.title: The Lawyers' Movement
events.subtitle: Musharraf suspends the Chief Justice
events.body: Musharraf suspends Chief Justice Iftikhar Chaudhry. Black-suited lawyers march, are beaten, march again. The movement will, within a year, bring Musharraf down.

events.title: Benazir shot in Rawalpindi
events.subtitle: Liaquat Bagh, again
events.body: Benazir — returned from exile two months earlier — is killed leaving a campaign rally at the same park where Liaquat died 56 years before. The PPP wins the February election in a wave of sympathy.

events.title: The 18th Amendment
events.subtitle: Devolution to the provinces
events.body: Parliament strips the president of the power to dismiss the government, devolves major subjects to the provinces, and restores the 1973 constitution's parliamentary spirit. The most consequential civilian constitutional reform since 1973.

events.title: Osama bin Laden killed in Abbottabad
events.subtitle: A compound in Bilal Town
events.body: SEAL Team Six lands two modified helicopters at a compound a short walk from the Pakistan Military Academy, kills bin Laden, and leaves. Pakistan's government learns of it as the helicopters are leaving its airspace. The military's credibility, at home and abroad, collapses simultaneously.

events.title: Army Public School, Peshawar
events.subtitle: 149 dead, 132 of them children
events.body: Seven TTP gunmen walk into the APS auditorium and open fire on schoolchildren. The country convulses. The National Action Plan follows — military courts, the reopening of executions, a concerted army push in North Waziristan.

events.title: Imran Khan, Prime Minister
events.subtitle: Twenty-two years in opposition end
events.body: After 22 years of campaigning, Imran Khan's PTI wins a plurality. A cricketing hero, a philanthropist, a populist — and, his critics insist, the candidate of the establishment. His term will end, four years later, in a vote of no-confidence.

events.title: Imran ousted
events.subtitle: The first Pakistani PM removed by no-confidence
events.body: Imran becomes the first Pakistani PM removed by a successful no-confidence vote. He alleges a US-backed conspiracy. Shehbaz Sharif — Nawaz's brother — takes over. The establishment and Imran, once close, become open enemies.

events.title: Economic crisis, IMF again
events.subtitle: Reserves at three weeks
events.body: The rupee collapses. Reserves cover three weeks of imports. Pakistan's 23rd IMF programme is signed — more tightly conditioned than any before. A now-familiar pattern: borrow, austerity, a brief rally, a new crisis.

events.title: May 9
events.subtitle: Rangers arrest Imran; PTI crowds attack army sites
events.body: Paramilitary Rangers drag Imran from the Islamabad High Court premises. Within hours, PTI supporters attack the Jinnah House in Lahore — the Corps Commander's residence — and other military installations. The army responds with arrests that run into the thousands.

events.title: The 2024 election
events.subtitle: PTI candidates run as independents — and win
events.body: Denied its electoral symbol, the PTI runs its candidates as independents — and they win the largest single bloc. A PML-N / PPP coalition forms the government. Shehbaz Sharif returns as Prime Minister. Imran remains in prison.
